title: Opportunity Cost & Why Ads Work on You
summary: Every purchase has a hidden cost, and advertising is built to make you forget it.
doThisToday: Before your next purchase over $20, ask out loud: "what am I giving up to buy this?"

cards.prompt: Opportunity cost: whatever you give up by choosing one thing over another.
cards.explanation: Money you spend on one thing is money you can't spend on anything else. That's not just a budgeting rule — it's math you can't escape, no matter how much you earn.

cards.prompt: You're saving for a $1,200 used car. You spend $150 on new AirPods Pro instead of saving it. What's the real opportunity cost?
options: You're now $150 further from your car, meaning more weeks of work before you can buy it
AirPods have no opportunity cost since you'll use them daily
cards.explanation: The AirPods aren't just $150 — they're $150 worth of progress toward your car goal, gone. That's the real price, even though the sticker only said $150.

cards.prompt: Companies spend billions of dollars specifically to make you forget about opportunity cost.
cards.explanation: Advertising isn't neutral information — it's designed, tested, and optimized to make you want things, often faster than you can think it through.

cards.prompt: True or false: ads mostly just tell you facts about a product so you can make an informed choice.
cards.explanation: Some ads do inform, but most are built around emotion — making you feel like you're missing out, falling behind, or not enough without the product. Facts are often secondary to feelings.

cards.prompt: You see "Only 3 left in stock — order in the next 10 minutes!" on a website. What's this tactic designed to do?
options: Give you an honest inventory update
Create urgency so you buy before thinking it through
cards.explanation: Scarcity and countdown timers short-circuit your ability to pause and think. Sometimes the stock claim is even true — but the tactic exists specifically to rush your decision either way.

cards.prompt: Which of these is a common advertising trick aimed at teens specifically?
options: Showing influencers you follow using a product to make it feel like a must-have
cards.explanation: Social proof from people you already trust or admire is one of the most effective tactics out there — it doesn't feel like an ad, which is exactly why it works so well.

cards.prompt: Sort each into Real Information or Persuasion Trick.
cards.explanation: Real information helps you compare and decide. Persuasion tricks are built to create urgency or emotion so you decide faster — before you've actually compared anything.

cards.prompt: Stores design their layout to trigger impulse buys, on purpose.
cards.frontText: Why is candy almost always placed right next to the checkout line?
cards.backText: It's deliberate retail psychology — checkout lines create idle waiting time, and small, cheap, craveable items placed right there trigger impulse grabs. You weren't planning to buy it when you walked in; the placement did the convincing.
cards.explanation: The same principle shows up online: recommended add-ons at checkout, "customers also bought," and one-click upsells are the digital version of the candy aisle.

cards.prompt: None of this means ads are evil or that wants are bad.
cards.explanation: It just means: every time something feels like a must-have right now, that urgency might be doing its job on purpose. Naming that gives you back the choice.
